1: <header>Environmental sustainability assessment of pig manure mono- and co-digestion and dynamic land application of the digestate </header>
Yizhen Zhang a, Yan , Jiang , Shun Wang a,b , Zhongzhong Wang a, , Yanchen Liu Zhenhu Hu Xinmin Zhan

<list>a Department of Civil Engineering, College of Engineering and Informatics, National University of Ireland Galway, Ireland 
b MaREI, The SFI Research Centre for Energy, Climate and Marine, Ireland 
C State Key Joint Laboratory of Environment Simulation and Pollution Control, School of Environment, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China 
d School of Civil Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, 230009, Anhui Province, China 
e Ryan Institute, National University of Ireland Galway, Ireland</list>

ARTICLEINE|ABSTRACT Keywords: Digestate management Emission inventory Grass silage Life cycle assessment (LCA) Nutrient availability Soil index|In this study, the environmental impacts of pig manure direct land application and anaerobic digestion (mono- and co-digestion) for biomethane production followed by digestate land application were evaluated by life cycle assessment, with a focus on the dynamic land application of digestate in terms of the nutrient profiles, soil nutrient status and environmental regulations. Ireland was used as a case study where co-digestion of manure with grass silage is viable, and an Irish emission inventory was established. The results indicate that pig manure and grass silage co-digestion performs best in 9 of the 11 impact categories assessed. Compared with direct land application, mono-digestion of pig manure decreases direct greenhouse gas emissions by 48% (190 tonne CO2e). Co-digestion with grass silage increases the total energy recovery by 226% (1592 MWh) than mono-digestion. The nitrogen available for plants in digestate (41.8%) is a little lower than that in raw pig manure (43.2%) due to the higher ammonia emissions after anaerobic degradation. The environmental impacts of the three pig manure management methods are greatly affected by soil nutrient conditions and regional environmental reg- ulations, which determine the organic fertilizer application rates and consequently the required land areas and transport distances. In this case study, the land application area for digestate is determined by phosphorus acceptance of soils rather than nitrogen or potassium. This study provides practical insights to farmers, the gas industry, and policy makers to conduct effective organic fertilizer application, select optimal AD plant location, and maximize environmental benefits.
2: Globally, 346 million tonnes of meat was consumed in 2018, and pork accounted for 34.6% [1]. The global pork consumption was esti- mated to rise by 13% and 22% in 2030 and 2050 compared with the 2020 level, respectively [2]. The high demand for pork production leads to a pig manure (PM) generation of 1.7 billion tonnes annually in the world [3]. In Ireland, pig production ranks the third in the economic value of the agri-food industry, with a total pig number of 1.62 million and the PM production of 3.2 million tonnes per year [4,5]. Direct land application of PM is the most widely used management method due to its easy operation and low cost [6]. However, the improper application

of PM may cause serious environmental problems, e.g. nutrient leaching, ammonia and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and pathogen contam- ination [7,8]. It thereby necessitates alternative management strategies. Anaerobic digestion (AD) provides a promising alternative PM management method for indoor intensive pig farms, where heat is required for the pig houses and PM is generated and collected throughout the year. The organic carbon (C) is recovered as renewable biogas and utilized by combined heat and power (CHP), and the nutri- ents (potassium, K; phosphorus, P; and nitrogen, N) in the digestate can be applied on land. But the economic viability of PM mono-digestion has been questioned due to the ammonia inhibition and low biogas yield, while co-digestion with energy crops, agricultural by-products and food waste is more economic viable due to the enhanced biogas production

[6,9]. But the environmental impacts of the co-digestion systems vary according to the characteristics and availability of the co-substrates, pre-treatment processes, and the development of the region [10]. The easily accessible grass silage (GS) in Ireland may provide a good co-substrate because GS requires a low amount of fertilizer and little irrigation, and its collection does not cause habitat destruction and land use change [11]. Xie et al. [12] indicated that the addition of GS to PM mono-digestion could overcome the ammonia inhibition and increase the specific methane yield by 63%. However, the introduction of GS into digesters involves extra processes like harvesting, transportation and pre-treatment, which would increase GHG emissions and fuel con- sumptions. Whether the increased energy production can offset these negative impacts should be evaluated. The life cycle assessment (LCA) on co-digestion of GS with animal manure has been conducted in a few studies. But these studies focused on different aspects, for example, Pehme et al. [13] emphasized the importance of grass types (semi-- natural grass and cultivated grass) on environmental impacts. Tsapekos et al. [14] focused on the effects of different GS harvesting machines (Disc-mower and Excoriator) and biogas utilization methods (CHP and transportation fuel) on environmental performance. De Vries et al. [15] assessed the environmental impacts caused by providing a substitute for the initial use of digestated grass, including the land use changes. However, how the digestate nutrient profile and application strategy influence the sustainability of the PM and GS co-digestion concept has not been studied.

The down-stream digestate management is a critical concern to AD. Direct land application of digestate is widely used due to its low cost compared with other technologies like liquid-solid separation, com- posting or pyrolysis for the solid fraction, and wastewater treatment for the liquid fraction [16-19]. In Ireland, the operational cost of liquid-solid separation is estimated to be 12.8/m3, much higher than the costs of € 4.9/m³ of direct land application and 5.2/m3 of AD [6]. The additional costs of the following liquid aerobic treatment and solid composting are estimated to be 4.6/m3 and 2.8/m³ respectively [6]. Even though the cost of solid pyrolysis can be minimized by using sur- plus heat in the AD plant to almost zero [20], the costs of separation and liquid treatment are inevitable. Thereby land application after AD is selected for assessment in this study. The nutrient profile of the digestate differs from that of raw substrate due to the degradation of organic wastes during AD [21], which results in different emissions in the following storage and land application processes. For example, the ammonia (NH3) emission during digestate storage is 50% higher than that of raw PM storage [22]. These consequently lead to different nu- trients available for plants. Therefore, a detailed assessment of nutrient availability of the PM and digestate is required.

Apart from nutrient profiles, the application of PM or digestate on land is also affected by soil nutrient status (barren or fertile), nutrient requirements of plants and local environmental regulations. The fertility index of the soil varies greatly in different countries. For example, the medium/moderate soil P index is 5-10 mg/L in Ireland [23], but the

values are 10-20 mg/L in Cameroon [24] and 16-25 mg/L in the United Kingdom [25]. Soils with low P status generally have fast and large P fixation capacity, thus the fertilizer P utilized by the plant is low [26]. So in low P status soils, it is recommended to supply 50% of plant required P from organic manure in Ireland, with the remaining 50% from chemical fertilizer [26,27]. The case of K is similar. The EU Nitrates Directive (91/676/EEC) requires N applied from organic fertilizer must not exceed 170 kg N/ha/yr to protect the water against the contami- nation of nitrate. The restriction of nutrient application rate affects the land area, transportation distance, chemical fertilizer requirement, and subsequently the total economic cost and environmental sustainability of the management system. Considering the complex and multi-dimensional conditions, a comprehensive assessment is necessary, and a dynamic land use strategy is required.

In this study, Ireland was selected as a case study to assess different PM management and organic fertilizer application strategies. The methodology used in this study allows generalization of the findings by utilizing localized inventory, soil nutrient conditions and environmental regulations. The life cycle assessment (LCA) was conducted to 3 PM management scenarios: (1) direct land application of PM on farm; (2) mono-digestion of PM and land application of the digestate; and (3) co- digestion of PM with GS and land application of the digestate. The ob- jectives of this study were: (1) to compare the environmental impacts of the 3 PM management methods and determine a sustainable option; (2) to assess the nutrient availability of raw PM and digestates; and (3) to provide proper land application strategies in terms of the nutrient pro- files, plant requirements, soil status and environmental regulations.

[14] focused on the effects of different GS harvesting machines (Disc-mower and Excoriator) and biogas utilization methods (CHP and transportation fuel) on environmental performance. De Vries et al. [15] assessed the environmental impacts caused by providing a substitute for the initial use of digestated grass, including the land use changes. However, how the digestate nutrient profile and application strategy influence the sustainability of the PM and GS co-digestion concept has not been studied.

The down-stream digestate management is a critical concern to AD. Direct land application of digestate is widely used due to its low cost compared with other technologies like liquid-solid separation, com- posting or pyrolysis for the solid fraction, and wastewater treatment for the liquid fraction [16-19]. In Ireland, the operational cost of liquid-solid separation is estimated to be 12.8/m3, much higher than the costs of € 4.9/m³ of direct land application and 5.2/m3 of AD [6]. The additional costs of the following liquid aerobic treatment and solid composting are estimated to be 4.6/m3 and 2.8/m³ respectively [6]. Even though the cost of solid pyrolysis can be minimized by using sur- plus heat in the AD plant to almost zero [20], the costs of separation and liquid treatment are inevitable. Thereby land application after AD is selected for assessment in this study. The nutrient profile of the digestate differs from that of raw substrate due to the degradation of organic wastes during AD [21], which results in different emissions in the following storage and land application processes. For example, the ammonia (NH3) emission during digestate storage is 50% higher than that of raw PM storage [22]. These consequently lead to different nu- trients available for plants. Therefore, a detailed assessment of nutrient availability of the PM and digestate is required.

Apart from nutrient profiles, the application of PM or digestate on land is also affected by soil nutrient status (barren or fertile), nutrient requirements of plants and local environmental regulations. The fertility index of the soil varies greatly in different countries. For example, the medium/moderate soil P index is 5-10 mg/L in Ireland [23], but the

values are 10-20 mg/L in Cameroon [24] and 16-25 mg/L in the United Kingdom [25]. Soils with low P status generally have fast and large P fixation capacity, thus the fertilizer P utilized by the plant is low [26]. So in low P status soils, it is recommended to supply 50% of plant required P from organic manure in Ireland, with the remaining 50% from chemical fertilizer [26,27]. The case of K is similar. The EU Nitrates Directive (91/676/EEC) requires N applied from organic fertilizer must not exceed 170 kg N/ha/yr to protect the water against the contami- nation of nitrate. The restriction of nutrient application rate affects the land area, transportation distance, chemical fertilizer requirement, and subsequently the total economic cost and environmental sustainability of the management system. Considering the complex and multi-dimensional conditions, a comprehensive assessment is necessary, and a dynamic land use strategy is required.

In this study, Ireland was selected as a case study to assess different PM management and organic fertilizer application strategies. The methodology used in this study allows generalization of the findings by utilizing localized inventory, soil nutrient conditions and environmental regulations. The life cycle assessment (LCA) was conducted to 3 PM management scenarios: (1) direct land application of PM on farm; (2) mono-digestion of PM and land application of the digestate; and (3) co- digestion of PM with GS and land application of the digestate. The ob- jectives of this study were: (1) to compare the environmental impacts of the 3 PM management methods and determine a sustainable option; (2) to assess the nutrient availability of raw PM and digestates; and (3) to provide proper land application strategies in terms of the nutrient pro- files, plant requirements, soil status and environmental regulations.
7: Globally, 346 million tonnes of meat was consumed in 2018, and pork accounted for 34.6% [1]. The global pork consumption was esti- mated to rise by 13% and 22% in 2030 and 2050 compared with the 2020 level, respectively [2]. The high demand for pork production leads to a pig manure (PM) generation of 1.7 billion tonnes annually in the world [3]. In Ireland, pig production ranks the third in the economic value of the agri-food industry, with a total pig number of 1.62 million and the PM production of 3.2 million tonnes per year [4,5]. Direct land application of PM is the most widely used management method due to its easy operation and low cost [6]. However, the improper application

of PM may cause serious environmental problems, e.g. nutrient leaching, ammonia and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and pathogen contam- ination [7,8]. It thereby necessitates alternative management strategies. Anaerobic digestion (AD) provides a promising alternative PM management method for indoor intensive pig farms, where heat is required for the pig houses and PM is generated and collected throughout the year. The organic carbon (C) is recovered as renewable biogas and utilized by combined heat and power (CHP), and the nutri- ents (potassium, K; phosphorus, P; and nitrogen, N) in the digestate can be applied on land. But the economic viability of PM mono-digestion has been questioned due to the ammonia inhibition and low biogas yield, while co-digestion with energy crops, agricultural by-products and food waste is more economic viable due to the enhanced biogas production

The methodology used in this study allows generalization of the findings by utilizing localized inventory, soil nutrient conditions and environmental regulations. The life cycle assessment (LCA) was conducted to 3 PM management scenarios: (1) direct land application of PM on farm; (2) mono-digestion of PM and land application of the digestate; and (3) co- digestion of PM with GS and land application of the digestate. 10: The average pig herd size is 728 SOWS in Ireland [28], with an average PM production of 20.7 m³ per SOW per year [6]. Thus totally 15, 070 m³ PM is produced annually. The PM is transported for land application after storage. During co-digestion of PM and GS, the two substrates are mixed at a volatile solids (VS) ratio of 1:1 for the highest biomethane yield [12], and the corresponding GS needed is 1347 t/yr. In Ireland, the GS is cut for 1-3 times per year as needed. A GS yield of 5-6 dry matter (DM)/ha can be reached in the first cut in mid-May and a second cut of 5-6 DM/ha can be reached in June July [29,30]. The GS silage yield of 10 t DM/ha/yr is used in this study, and therefore a grass land area of 42.8 ha is needed for providing the GS. The function unit is the PM produced from a baseline pig farm of 728 SOWS annually and the corresponding GS needed, i.e. 15,070 m³ PM/yr and 1347 t GS/yr, respectively.

12: The PM management methods assessed in this study include PM

mono-digestion, PM and GS co-digestion, and land application of raw PM and digestates. Fig. 1 illustrates the system boundaries of the three scenarios, include: (1) GS cultivation, shredding and pasteurisation; (2) storage of PM, GS and digestate; (3) PM mono-digestion, and PM and GS co-digestion; (4) biogas utilization by CHP; (5) transportation of PM, GS and digestate; and (6) land application of PM and digestate. The back- ground system includes substituted production of heat, electricity, diesel, water and chemical fertilizers.
13: The principles and framework for LCA are described in ISO-14040 standard [31]. SimaPro is used in this study with Ecoinvent 3.5 as the primary database and IPCC data for greenhouse gases. The environ- mental impacts are assessed using CML-IA baseline methodology. The impacts assessed include global warming potential (GWP), fossil fuel depletion potential (FDP), acidification potential, eutrophication po- tential, photochemical oxidation, ozone layer depletion potential, human toxicity, terrestrial ecotoxicity, marine aquatic ecotoxicity, freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity, and abiotic depletion of elements. Ac- cording to the CML-IA baseline method, the impact factors of NH3 for human toxicity and acidification are 0.1 and 1.6, respectively; the impact factors of nitrous oxide (N20) and methane (CH4) to GWP are 265 and 28, respectively; eutrophication effects of NH3, N2O and nitrate (NO) leaching to freshwater are 0.35, 0.27 and 0.1, respectively [32].
14: A detailed Ireland-based inventory is collected as much as possible for practical PM treatment assessment. The characteristics of PM and GS, and performances on PM mono-digestion, and PM and GS co-digestion are based on the research of Xie et al. [12]. The GHG emissions for treatment of PM and GS include CH4 and N20. The carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions during manure management are neglected as they are considered biogenetic [33].
15: Pig farms are required to have 26 weeks' storage capacities in Ireland

to make sure that PM will not be spread on land during the prohibited spreading period and in forbidden weather or ground conditions [34]. The same storage capacity is applied for digestate. The CH4 emission factor for storage of PM is calculated according to IPCC guidelines [33]. EFCH4 = VS X X 0.67kg/m3 X MCF X MS (1)

where EFCH4 is the emission factor of CH4 during PM storage, kg CH4/ sow/yr; VS is volatile solids excreted by breeding pigs on farm, kg VS/ sow/d; BO is the maximum PM methane production capacity, m³ CH4/kg VS, which is 0.26 m³ CH4/kg VS based on Dennehy et al. [35]; 0.67 is the factor converting CH4 from m³ to kg; MCF is the manure methane conversion factor during storage, %, which is 10% at an annual average temperature of 10 °C in Ireland; and MS is the fraction of PM handled by storage, dimensionless (supposed to be 100% in Scenario 1).
The EFCH4 calculated from Eq. (1) is 9.38 kg CH4/sow/yr, corre- sponding to 15.8% of the total CH4 produced from mono-digestion of PM based on Xie et al. [12].

The N2O emissions occur directly and indirectly. Direct N2O emission occurs via nitrification and denitrification of N contained in the manure. Indirect N2O emission results from the deposition of N volatilised in forms of NO and NH3, and NO3 losses through runoff and leaching [33]. The direct N2O emission factor from raw manure storage is 0.38% of the total N [37], and the average NH3 emission factor is 10.5% of the total N in storage [37,38]. The nutrient leaching during PM storage in the concrete tank is assumed negligible [15]. The N2O emission factor from atmospheric deposition is 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg N, and from leaching/- runoff is 0.0075 kg N2O-N/kg N, respectively [39].
16: Pig farms are required to have 26 weeks' storage capacities in Ireland

to make sure that PM will not be spread on land during the prohibited spreading period and in forbidden weather or ground conditions [34]. The same storage capacity is applied for digestate. The CH4 emission factor for storage of PM is calculated according to IPCC guidelines [33]. EFCH4 = VS X X 0.67kg/m3 X MCF X MS (1)

where EFCH4 is the emission factor of CH4 during PM storage, kg CH4/ sow/yr; VS is volatile solids excreted by breeding pigs on farm, kg VS/ sow/d; BO is the maximum PM methane production capacity, m³ CH4/kg VS, which is 0.26 m³ CH4/kg VS based on Dennehy et al. [35]; 0.67 is the factor converting CH4 from m³ to kg; MCF is the manure methane conversion factor during storage, %, which is 10% at an annual average temperature of 10 °C in Ireland; and MS is the fraction of PM handled by storage, dimensionless (supposed to be 100% in Scenario 1).
The EFCH4 calculated from Eq. (1) is 9.38 kg CH4/sow/yr, corre- sponding to 15.8% of the total CH4 produced from mono-digestion of PM based on Xie et al. [12].

The N2O emissions occur directly and indirectly. Direct N2O emission occurs via nitrification and denitrification of N contained in the manure. Indirect N2O emission results from the deposition of N volatilised in forms of NO and NH3, and NO3 losses through runoff and leaching [33]. The direct N2O emission factor from raw manure storage is 0.38% of the total N [37], and the average NH3 emission factor is 10.5% of the total N in storage [37,38]. The nutrient leaching during PM storage in the concrete tank is assumed negligible [15]. The N2O emission factor from atmospheric deposition is 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg N, and from leaching/- runoff is 0.0075 kg N2O-N/kg N, respectively [39].
17: 2.4.2.1. GS cultivation, storage and mixing with PM. The GHG emission during the storage of GS is little. The investigation from eight biogas plants reported the CH4 and NH3 emission factors were as low as 1.25 10-3 g CH4/kWh and 2.52 X 10-3 g NH3/kWh electricity produced by CHP, respectively, and the N2O emission was not detected [40]. Thus, the GHG emitted from GS storage is neglected in this study. The CH4 emission factor from the mixing tank is 0.05% of the total CH4 [40]. The

"1 2 3"|PM storage Transport Land application "1 2 3"|CHP Mono-digestion Transport of PM Storage Land application "1 2 3"|CHP Co-Digestion Transport Land application of PM & GS Storage & GS cultivation Mixing Pasteurization Shredding Storage Transport
Fig. 1. System boundaries of three pig manure management systems.

distance for transporting GS to the AD plant is assumed to be 10 km [41]. The agricultural inputs for grass include seed, fertilizer, herbicide, lime, and diesel for transportation and cultivation, with their inventories gathered from the Ecoinvent database [32]. Diesel consumptions for GS cultivation, including energy inputs for ploughing, sowing, harrowing, rolling and harvesting, are obtained from Smyth et al. [11]. The mass loss during storage and transportation is assumed at 8% [42].

2.4.2.2. Anaerobic digester, biogas utilization and digestate storage. Anaerobic digestion is conducted in continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTR) at 35 °C with the hydraulic retention time (HRT) of 30 days [12]. The specific methane yields (SMYs) for mono- co-digestion are 154 and 251 L CH4/g VSadded [12], leading to the total CH4 productions of 60,399 and 196,688 /yr, respectively. The average fugitive emission of the digester is 1.5% of the total CH4 production [15,41,43]. The NH3 and N2O emissions from anaerobic digesters are negligible [40].

The biogas is utilized by CHP to produce electricity and heat. The life cycle inventories for electricity production in Ireland and heat produc- tion from natural gas are gathered from the Ecoinvent database [32]. The CH4 emission in the CHP unit may occur due to incomplete com- bustion of CH4 and leakages from the engine and gas pipes. The average CH4 and N2O emission factors are 1.89% of the total CH4 and 0.018 g N2O/kWh electricity produced, respectively [40,44]. The energy con- tent of CH4 is 35.7 MJ/m³, while 3.6 MJ of energy equals to 1 kWh of electricity. The CHP efficiencies for electricity and heat are 40% and 45%, respectively [9].

The CH4 emission factor is ~1.0% of the total CH4 produced during an 81-day post-storage of the digestate [36]. The NH3 emission during digestate storage is 50% higher than that of raw PM [22], with an emission factor of 15.8% of the total N in storage. The N2O emission during the storage of digestate is not detected [36,40].

20: The input energy during AD includes electricity and heat. The elec- tricity demand includes pumping, mixing and the consumptions for driving CHP and pasteurisation. In Ireland, the electricity consumptions for on farm mono- and co-digestion systems are 5.4 and 7.0 kWh/m³ substrate, respectively [6,9]. The heat demand includes the energy consumptions for maintaining the temperature of AD and the heat for pasteurisation. The pasteurisation is introduced to avoid cross contam- ination of imported raw feedstock materials [45]. In Ireland, pasteur- isation process is not applied if the PM and GS come from the same farm where the AD plant is located [45]. But in case there is not enough GS in the AD plant based farm and GS needs to be transported from other grasslands, the pasteurisation of GS is considered here. The daily heat demand is calculated according to Nolan et al. [6].
21: The PM and digestate are applied on grassland as substitutes for common chemical fertilizers used in Ireland, i.e. calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN), single superphosphate, and potassium chloride [41]. The inventory for the production of chemical fertilizer is gathered from the Ecoinvent database [32]. The soil organic carbon stock increased by PM/digestate application is not considered in the current study. Although the direct application of PM can alter soil properties and enhance soil carbon sequestration, the climate change mitigation effect is estimated to be offset by stimulated N2O and CH4 emissions from the soil [46]. A distance of 10 km was considered for transporting PM and digestate to the application field [9,41]. About 80% of the total N in the PM digestate is in the form of NH4-N [38]. The total N contents in the digestate are calculated to be 2.90 and 3.18 kg/m³ for mono- and co-digestion, respectively, based on NH4-N contents reported by Xie et al. [12]. The P and K contents are calculated based on the average concentrations in PM and GS [12,30,38,47,48]. As a result, the average

concentrations of P and K are 0.75 and 2.44 kg/m³ in mono-digestate, and 0.76 and 2.71 kg/m³ in co-digestate, respectively. The nutrient contents of raw PM are assumed the same with mono-digestate consid- ering no losses during AD.

The CH4 emission factor for raw PM land application is 0.02 kg CH4-C/kg N applied [49], and is neglected for digestate land application [36]. The average NH3 emission factor during raw PM land application is 27% of total N applied [38,50], which has no significant difference from the digestate [22]. The average direct N2O emission factor for raw PM is 0.46% of the total applied N [51,52], with the value of digestate land application 25% less at 0.34% of the total applied N [22]. The reduction for digestate application is because lower organic matter af- fects infiltration of digestate in the soil, and the decreased microbial demand for oxygen reduces heterotrophic denitrification [36]. The average N loss by leaching and runoff following land application of PM is 23% of total applied N in Ireland [51], and the same value is used for digestate. The average NH3, direct and indirect N2O emission factors for CAN application are 4%, 1.75% and 0.03% of the total N applied, respectively [49,53]. In this study, the losses of P and K to the surface and ground water are not considered, as their losses depend mainly on soil status and the contents in the soil rather than the amount applied by fertilizers [54]. WRAP [55] also reports the cumulative leaching of P is low after land application of livestock slurry and manure-based diges- tate, with no significant difference with the untreated control.

24: The sensitivities of GWP and FDP for the Co-AD scenario caused by the variations of different parameters on are analysed. A +10% variation is applied to each parameter with the other parameters constant at default assumptions described previously. The parameters tested include electricity input for AD, heat input for AD, transportation dis- tance from pig farm to land application field, emission factors for NH3, CH4 and N2O during digestate storage and land application, CH4 yield and CH4 fugitive emission from AD, and nutrient contents in the feed- stock. Parameters causing more than 1% changes in the results with are reported.
25: <header>3.1. Direct GHG emissions and nutrient availability </header>

The air emissions and nutrient losses occurring in the three scenarios are shown in Fig. 2. Direct GHG emissions in each process are calculated as the sum of N2O emissions, and CH4 emission. The total direct GHG emissions from the Baseline, Mono-AD, and Co-AD scenarios are 398, 208, and 413 tonne CO2e, respectively. Compared with the Baseline, Mono-AD decreases the direct GHG emissions by 48% due to the re- covery of CH4. Co-AD has higher direct GHG emissions because of the additional C and N from GS. The compositions of direct GHG emissions and contributions of difference processes vary greatly in different sce- narios. In Baseline scenario, PM storage is the most environment impacting process, leading to 70% of the total direct GHG emissions. The CH4 and N2O emissions account for 53% and 47% of the total direct emissions, respectively. While in Mono-AD, N2O emission accounts for 72% of the direct GHG emissions, with CH4 proportion being 28%. It indicates the recovery of organic C in the form of CH4 during AD can greatly reduce GHG emissions. Land spreading of digestate becomes the most environment affecting process, contributing to 58% of the total direct GHG emissions. In Co-AD scenario, CH4 and N2O emissions ac- count for 46% and 54% of the direct GHG emissions, respectively. The increase in CH4 proportion than Mono-AD is due to the great addition of organic C from GS (indicating by 68% increase in CH4 yield) relative to little addition of N (indicating by 10% increase in total N). The main emissions come from AD processes (including CHP) and digestate land spreading, accounting for 38% and 35% of the direct GHG emissions,

The available fertilizer indicates the nutrients able to be retained in the soil. The available N retained in soil is 44.2%, 41.8% and 41.8% of the feedstock N in Baseline, Mono-AD, and Co-AD scenarios, respec- tively. Even though digestate contains higher concentration of biologi- cally available N than raw PM due to the degradation of organic N [12, 56], the subsequent higher NH3 emission offsets the availability. The major N losses in all scenarios come from land spreading, with NH3 emission and NO3 leaching accounting for about 24% and 20% of the total feedstock N, respectively. As no losses are assumed for P and K during the processes, 100% of P and K are retained in the soil as avail- able nutrients.

The major N losses in all scenarios come from land spreading, with NH3 emission and NO3 leaching accounting for about 24% and 20% of the total feedstock N, respectively. As no losses are assumed for P and K during the processes, 100% of P and K are retained in the soil as avail- able nutrients.
28: <header>3.1. Direct GHG emissions and nutrient availability </header>

The air emissions and nutrient losses occurring in the three scenarios are shown in Fig. 2. Direct GHG emissions in each process are calculated as the sum of N2O emissions, and CH4 emission. The total direct GHG emissions from the Baseline, Mono-AD, and Co-AD scenarios are 398, 208, and 413 tonne CO2e, respectively. Compared with the Baseline, Mono-AD decreases the direct GHG emissions by 48% due to the re- covery of CH4. Co-AD has higher direct GHG emissions because of the additional C and N from GS. The compositions of direct GHG emissions and contributions of difference processes vary greatly in different sce- narios. In Baseline scenario, PM storage is the most environment impacting process, leading to 70% of the total direct GHG emissions. The CH4 and N2O emissions account for 53% and 47% of the total direct emissions, respectively. While in Mono-AD, N2O emission accounts for 72% of the direct GHG emissions, with CH4 proportion being 28%. It indicates the recovery of organic C in the form of CH4 during AD can greatly reduce GHG emissions. Land spreading of digestate becomes the most environment affecting process, contributing to 58% of the total direct GHG emissions. In Co-AD scenario, CH4 and N2O emissions ac- count for 46% and 54% of the direct GHG emissions, respectively. The increase in CH4 proportion than Mono-AD is due to the great addition of organic C from GS (indicating by 68% increase in CH4 yield) relative to little addition of N (indicating by 10% increase in total N). The main emissions come from AD processes (including CHP) and digestate land spreading, accounting for 38% and 35% of the direct GHG emissions,

31: The normalized midpoint results of 11 impact categories assessed are shown in Fig. 3. The Baseline scenario has the highest impacts in 8 of the 11 impact categories, while Co-AD scenario has the lowest damage po- tentials in 9 of them, due to the high biogas credit. The higher acidifi- cation and eutrophication potentials in Mono-AD and Co-AD scenarios are caused by high NH3 emissions from digestate storage and land spreading. Energy consumptions for AD provide major contributions to human- and eco-toxicity categories. Direct GHG emissions impose dominate impacts on the GWP, acidification, and eutrophication cate- gories. Apart from energy consumptions in AD, GS production provides additional contribution to fossil fuel depletion, photochemical oxida- tion, abiotic depletion and ozone depletion damages; while trans- portation imposes significant damage to ozone depletion potential. However, the high displacement credit of biogas in Co-AD system leads to high reduction potentials for most environmental impact categories, and the replacement of chemical fertilizer further compensates the damages from GWP, FDP, photochemical oxidation and abiotic deple- tion of elements.

The process contributions to GWP and FDP are depicted in Fig. 4. The Co-AD scenario presents the lowest GWP and FDP due to the credits from biogas and organic fertilizer. Compared to the Baseline, Mono-AD and Co-AD reduce GWP by 95% and 149%, and reduce FDP by 30% and 328%, respectively. The results obtained in this study agree with other studies that mono-digestion of PM can reduce GWP and FDP effectively compared with direct land application [8], and co-digestion of PM with organic wastes performs better than mono-AD due to enhanced biogas recovery [15,57]. However, the specific values in different literature vary. For example, De Vries et al. [15] studied co-digestion of PM and roadside grass, and the GWP and FDP were -89 kg CO2/t and -1027
kg PO4e kg CFC-11e kg 1,4-DBe kg 1,4-DBe kg 1,4-DBe kg 1,4-DBe kg C2H4e kg Sbe

Fig. 3. Relative annual midpoint impacts of pig manure management systems, normalized with reference to the highest (100%) and the lowest (0%) impacts in each impact category. The highest and lowest values of each impact category are displayed on the top and at the bottom of horizontal axis. GWP 100 = global warming potential 100 years, FDP = fossil fuels depletion potential, AC = acidification, EU = eutrophication, ODP = ozone layer depletion potential, HTOX = human toxicity, FTOX = freshwater aquatic ecotoxicity, MTOX = marine aquatic ecotoxicity, TTOX = terrestrial ecotoxicity, PO = photochemical oxidation, ADE = abiotic depletion of elements.

Fig. 4. Process-based global warming potential (a) and fossil fuel depletion potential (b) of three pig manure management systems. Positive = generated impact; negative = mitigated impact. Error bars indicate the best and worst possible results of global warming potential and fossil fuel depletion potential by varying modelling parameters in reported ranges.

MJ/t feedstock, respectively. Vaneeckhaute et al. [57] reported the GWP and FDP of -79 kg CO2/t and -475 MJ/t, respectively, during co-digestion of PM with organic residues (slaughterhouse waste, food waste, and GS). Lovarelli et al. [58] and Lijó et al. [59] reported similar GWPs of -16 and -14 kg CO2e/t, respectively, for co-digestion of PM with energy crops (triticale and maize silages). The GWP and FDP in this study are -7 kg CO2/t -346 MJ/t, respectively. The differences are caused by different feedstocks, system boundaries, and emission factors.

Direct GHG emissions account for 80%, 62% and 58% of the net GWP (without biogas and fertilizer displacement credits) in the Baseline, Mono-AD and Co-AD scenarios, respectively. The hotspot analysis in- dicates that for the Baseline, the PM storage and land application ac- count for 65% and 30% of the GWP, respectively. For mono- and co- digestion systems, the GWP mainly comes from energy inputs during AD and N2O emission in land spreading. Compared to Co-AD system, Mono-AD produces less CH4 and N20 emissions from fugitive, digestate storage and land spreading, consumes less energy during AD, and avoids significant GHG emissions from GS production and pasteurisation. However, the high displacement credit from biogas in Co-AD system offsets all these negative impacts. For FDP, transportation accounts for

80% of the fossil energy consumption in the Baseline. For Mono-AD scenario, the energy inputs for AD and transportation account for 73% and 22% of the total fossil fuel consumption, respectively. For the Co-AD scenario, the energy inputs for AD (53%) and the diesel input for GS production (21%) are the major contributors to energy depletion potential.

The production of GS induces additional energy inputs for cultiva- tion and pasteurisation, and generates extra emissions. In Ireland, the production of GS requires relatively low maintenance, with organic nutrients being supplied by animal wastes and little irrigation due to high rainfall. The GS production in the Co-AD scenario contributes to 16% and 21% of the net GWP and FDP, respectively. The pasteurisation of GS contributes to 4% of the net GWP and 9% of the net FDP, respectively. However, compared to Mono-AD, Co-AD scenario in- creases the SMY by 63%, leading to a total biogas production increase of 226%. The biogas production offsets 94% and 190% of the GWP and FDP in Co-AD scenario, respectively, and thereby compensates the negative impacts.

The error bars illustrate the maximum-minimum ranges based on the variations of emission factors and PM properties reported in litera- ture. The variation of GWP is more dramatic in the Baseline due to the large fluctuations of different emission factors. It means with improper management practices, the Baseline scenario can reach obvious high GHG emissions. The biogas credits in Mono-AD and Co-AD scenarios can counteract the uncertainty resulting from emission variations. Mono-AD has a smaller range of variation for GWP compared to Co-AD scenario due to the less CH4 leakage during AD and less N2O emissions during land spreading. All three scenarios have the potential to achieve nega- tive GHG emissions. The negative ranges of FDPs and small variations demonstrate the robustness of energy self-sufficient capability for all the three scenarios. These results are mainly caused by the substitution for chemical fertilizers and natural gas.

It means with improper management practices, the Baseline scenario can reach obvious high GHG emissions. The biogas credits in Mono-AD and Co-AD scenarios can counteract the uncertainty resulting from emission variations. Mono-AD has a smaller range of variation for GWP compared to Co-AD scenario due to the less CH4 leakage during AD and less N2O emissions during land spreading. All three scenarios have the potential to achieve nega- tive GHG emissions. The negative ranges of FDPs and small variations demonstrate the robustness of energy self-sufficient capability for all the three scenarios. These results are mainly caused by the substitution for chemical fertilizers and natural gas.
34: The normalized midpoint results of 11 impact categories assessed are shown in Fig. 3. The Baseline scenario has the highest impacts in 8 of the 11 impact categories, while Co-AD scenario has the lowest damage po- tentials in 9 of them, due to the high biogas credit. The higher acidifi- cation and eutrophication potentials in Mono-AD and Co-AD scenarios are caused by high NH3 emissions from digestate storage and land spreading. Energy consumptions for AD provide major contributions to human- and eco-toxicity categories. Direct GHG emissions impose dominate impacts on the GWP, acidification, and eutrophication cate- gories. Apart from energy consumptions in AD, GS production provides additional contribution to fossil fuel depletion, photochemical oxida- tion, abiotic depletion and ozone depletion damages; while trans- portation imposes significant damage to ozone depletion potential. However, the high displacement credit of biogas in Co-AD system leads to high reduction potentials for most environmental impact categories, and the replacement of chemical fertilizer further compensates the damages from GWP, FDP, photochemical oxidation and abiotic deple- tion of elements.

The process contributions to GWP and FDP are depicted in Fig. 4. The Co-AD scenario presents the lowest GWP and FDP due to the credits from biogas and organic fertilizer. Compared to the Baseline, Mono-AD and Co-AD reduce GWP by 95% and 149%, and reduce FDP by 30% and 328%, respectively. The results obtained in this study agree with other studies that mono-digestion of PM can reduce GWP and FDP effectively compared with direct land application [8], and co-digestion of PM with organic wastes performs better than mono-AD due to enhanced biogas recovery [15,57]. However, the specific values in different literature vary. For example, De Vries et al. [15] studied co-digestion of PM and roadside grass, and the GWP and FDP were -89 kg CO2/t and -1027

[57] reported the GWP and FDP of -79 kg CO2/t and -475 MJ/t, respectively, during co-digestion of PM with organic residues (slaughterhouse waste, food waste, and GS). Lovarelli et al. [58] and Lijó et al. [59] reported similar GWPs of -16 and -14 kg CO2e/t, respectively, for co-digestion of PM with energy crops (triticale and maize silages). The GWP and FDP in this study are -7 kg CO2/t -346 MJ/t, respectively. The differences are caused by different feedstocks, system boundaries, and emission factors.

Direct GHG emissions account for 80%, 62% and 58% of the net GWP (without biogas and fertilizer displacement credits) in the Baseline, Mono-AD and Co-AD scenarios, respectively. The hotspot analysis in- dicates that for the Baseline, the PM storage and land application ac- count for 65% and 30% of the GWP, respectively. For mono- and co- digestion systems, the GWP mainly comes from energy inputs during AD and N2O emission in land spreading. Compared to Co-AD system, Mono-AD produces less CH4 and N20 emissions from fugitive, digestate storage and land spreading, consumes less energy during AD, and avoids significant GHG emissions from GS production and pasteurisation. However, the high displacement credit from biogas in Co-AD system offsets all these negative impacts. For FDP, transportation accounts for

80% of the fossil energy consumption in the Baseline. For Mono-AD scenario, the energy inputs for AD and transportation account for 73% and 22% of the total fossil fuel consumption, respectively. For the Co-AD scenario, the energy inputs for AD (53%) and the diesel input for GS production (21%) are the major contributors to energy depletion potential.

The production of GS induces additional energy inputs for cultiva- tion and pasteurisation, and generates extra emissions. In Ireland, the production of GS requires relatively low maintenance, with organic nutrients being supplied by animal wastes and little irrigation due to high rainfall. The GS production in the Co-AD scenario contributes to 16% and 21% of the net GWP and FDP, respectively. The pasteurisation of GS contributes to 4% of the net GWP and 9% of the net FDP, respectively. However, compared to Mono-AD, Co-AD scenario in- creases the SMY by 63%, leading to a total biogas production increase of 226%. The biogas production offsets 94% and 190% of the GWP and FDP in Co-AD scenario, respectively, and thereby compensates the negative impacts.

All three scenarios have the potential to achieve nega- tive GHG emissions. The negative ranges of FDPs and small variations demonstrate the robustness of energy self-sufficient capability for all the three scenarios. These results are mainly caused by the substitution for chemical fertilizers and natural gas.

38: <header>3.3. Sensitivity analysis for GWP and FDP </header>

To recognize the key processes exerting dominant influences on the key categories, Fig. 5 presents the sensitive analysis on GWP and FDP in Co-AD scenario. Sensitivity results are reported as percent changes caused by per 10% changes in parameter values. CH4 yield has the highest environmental benefits in GWP and FDP. Among the parameters tested, the GWP is most sensitive to CH4 yield, followed by N2O emis- sions during digestate storage and land application, heat input for AD, feedstock N content, and NH3 emission for land application (Fig. 5 a). The FDP is most sensitive to CH4 yield and AD heat input (Fig. 5 b).

A 10% increase in CH4 yield can result in 68% and 31% reductions in GWP and FDP from the Co-AD system, respectively. A 10% heat con- sumption reduction in AD can cause 14% and 6% reductions of GWP and FDP, respectively. A 10% increase of N content in feedstock increases GWP by 14% but decreases FDP by 3%. It is because the elevated N content in feedstock increases N losses in various processes and con- tributes to higher GWP; but the increased N in digestate increases the displacement value of fertilizers and reduces the FDP. The increased NH3 emission in land spreading does not affect GWP directly, but it can increase the indirect N2O emission and decrease the N availability for land application, which leads to a lower substitution value for chemical fertilizer. Thus a 10% increase in NH3 emission results in increased GWP (11%) and FDP (2%). The increases of N2O emissions by 10% in land spreading and digestate storage can cause 15% and 11% increases in GWP, respectively. Based on this sensitivity analysis, the environmental performance of the system can be effectively improved by increasing the CH4 yield, improving fertilizer availability of the digestate and reducing energy inputs for AD operation.
Fig. 5. Sensitivity analysis for global warming potential (a) and fossil fuel depletion potential (b) with 10% change in different parameters.

41: <header>4.1. Requirements of land area and chemical fertilizer </header>

Accurate land application of PM and digestate is essential, otherwise the surplus nutrients in the soil may leach to ground and surface water and cause pollution. To follow good management practice of PM and digestate, the following key factors must be considered: (1) the nutrient status of the organic fertilizer; (2) the nutrient status of the receiving soil; (3) nutrient requirements of the plant growing on the soil; and (4) the local environmental regulations for organic fertilizer application. The nutrient status of PM and digestate presented as available fertilizers are shown in Fig. 2. In Ireland, soils are classified into 4 indexes based on nutrient levels, with index 1 and 4 indicating the most barren and fertile soils, respectively. The P and K ranges under different soil indexes are shown in Table 1 [23]. As mentioned above, the PM and digestate will be applied on grassland, and the GS will be cut twice annually. The nutri- ents required for the two cuts of GS in different soil indexes are sum- marized in Table 1 [60]. The practical spreading of PM or digestate is restricted by organic fertilizer application regulations. The N applied from livestock manure must be < 170 kg N/ha/yr (Nitrates Directive 91/676/EEC). The release rates of P and K from organic manure are determined by the P and K status in soils. In Ireland, when the P status is low at index 1-2 soils, only 50% of P in organic manure is deemed
Table 1 Soil index and fertilizer requirements for grass silage.

Soil index|Index 1|Index 2|Index 3|Index 4 Soil P range (mg/L)|0.0-3.0|3.1-5.0|5.1-8.0| Soil K range (mg/L)|0-50|51-100|101-150| Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements N (kg/ha)|225|225|225|225 P (kg/ha)|50|40|30|0 K (kg/ha)|245|205|160|0

available in the year of application, therefore it is recommended to supply 50% of plant required P from organic manure, with the remaining 50% from chemical fertilizer [26,27]. Similarly, 75% of plant required K is recommended to be supplied by organic manure with the other 25% from chemical fertilizer [27]. Medium to high P and K status soils (index 3-4) have much lower capacity to retain P and K, and 100% of the P and K can be applied from organic manure [26,27].

The 1-4 soil indexes are used to calculate the land area required for spreading the PM or digestate. As comparison and reference for areas with no P and K application limitations, the 100% application of P and K from PM/digestate at index 1 and 2 soils are also considered. Five cases are developed based on soil index 1-3 to present how changes in soil fertility and application rates of P and K would affect the land area and chemical fertilizer requirements. The LCA analysis on 3 PM management scenarios in the previous sections are conducted based on the soil index 1, which is considered as the default case. Soil at index 4 requires zero P and K fertilizers, which is excluded in the analysis to avoid nutrient loss to the environment.

<list>1) Index 1 (Default): Receiving soil has low P and K status at index 1. The PM or digestate can be used to supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 100% of plant required P and K on the soil. This case assumes 10 km transportation distance between pig farm and the grassland receiving nutrient application. 
2) Index 1(A): The receiving soil is at P and K index 1. The PM or digestate can supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 50% of plant required P and 75% required K on the soil. The transportation distance is increased to 20 km due to the larger spreading farm area. 
3) Index 2: Receiving soil has low P and K status at index 2. The application of PM or digestate is set to supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 100% of the plant required P and K. The case assumes 15 km transportation distance between pig farm and the grassland. 
4) Index 2(A): Receiving soil is at P and K index 2. The application rates of N, P and K from PM or digestate are up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 50% of plant required P and 75% required K, respectively. The trans- portation distance is increased to 30 km due to the larger spreading farm area. 
5) Index 3: Receiving soil has medium P and K status at index 3. The PM or digestate can be used to supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 100% of plant required P and K. This case assumes 20 km transportation distance between pig farm and the grassland receiving nutrient application.</list>

For all the 5 cases, the rest nutrients required after PM or digestate application will be supplied by individual chemical fertilizers. The land areas required for PM or digestate application are calculated based on the N, P, K requirements of the grassland, of which the maximum value is selected to avoid pollution caused by the surplus of the other two nutrients. P is the limiting element in this study. The results are shown in Table 2.

Compared with soil index 1, the land application areas for soil in- dexes 2 and 3 increase by 25% and 67%, respectively, due to the reduced P requirements. The rising in land area results in great increase in chemical fertilizers. The chemical N and K requirements increase by 36%-41% and 11%-19% at index 2, and increase by 100%-109% and

Soil index case|Index 1|Index A|Index 2|Index 2 A|Index 3 Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha) Baseline|226|452|283|565|377 Mono-AD|226|452|283|565|377 Co-AD|248|497|310|621|414 Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr) Baseline|32|82|44|108|65 Mono-AD|33|83|45|109|66 Co-AD|34|90|48|118|71 Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr) Baseline|19|46|21|50|24 Mono-AD|19|46|21|50|24 Co-AD|16|47|19|51|22

26%-38% at index 3 than index 1. The Baseline and Mono-AD scenarios have similar land and chemical fertilizer requirements in all cases because of the similar nutrient contents in raw PM and mono-digestate. While in Co-AD scenario, the land requirement increases by about 10% due to the additional nutrients introduced by GS, in which 42.8 ha is required to supply GS for AD. If 100% of the required P can be supplied by PM or digestate, the chemical N fertilizer requirement in Co-AD scenario increases by 6%-9% correspondingly than the Baseline, but the required chemical K decreases by 8%-16% because of the supple- ment of relatively high K content in GS. When assuming only 50% of the plant required P and 75% of required K can be supplied from PM or digestate for indexes 1 and 2 soils, the application land areas in indexes 1 A and 2 A double due to the P limit. It causes much greater chemical fertilizer requirement. The chemical N and K for index 1 A increase by 152%-165% and 142%-194% than index 1, and the requirements for index 2 A increase by 142%-146% and 138%-168% than index 2.

<list>1) Index 1 (Default): Receiving soil has low P and K status at index 1. The PM or digestate can be used to supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 100% of plant required P and K on the soil. This case assumes 10 km transportation distance between pig farm and the grassland receiving nutrient application. 
2) Index 1(A): The receiving soil is at P and K index 1. The PM or digestate can supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 50% of plant required P and 75% required K on the soil. The transportation distance is increased to 20 km due to the larger spreading farm area. 
3) Index 2: Receiving soil has low P and K status at index 2. The application of PM or digestate is set to supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 100% of the plant required P and K. The case assumes 15 km transportation distance between pig farm and the grassland. 
4) Index 2(A): Receiving soil is at P and K index 2. The application rates of N, P and K from PM or digestate are up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 50% of plant required P and 75% required K, respectively. The trans- portation distance is increased to 30 km due to the larger spreading farm area. 
5) Index 3: Receiving soil has medium P and K status at index 3. The PM or digestate can be used to supply up to 170 kg N/ha/yr, 100% of plant required P and K. This case assumes 20 km transportation distance between pig farm and the grassland receiving nutrient application.</list>

For all the 5 cases, the rest nutrients required after PM or digestate application will be supplied by individual chemical fertilizers. The land areas required for PM or digestate application are calculated based on the N, P, K requirements of the grassland, of which the maximum value is selected to avoid pollution caused by the surplus of the other two nutrients. P is the limiting element in this study. The results are shown in Table 2.

Compared with soil index 1, the land application areas for soil in- dexes 2 and 3 increase by 25% and 67%, respectively, due to the reduced P requirements. The rising in land area results in great increase in chemical fertilizers. The chemical N and K requirements increase by 36%-41% and 11%-19% at index 2, and increase by 100%-109% and
Land area and nutrient requirements for PM or digestate spreading on different soil types in Ireland.

Soil index case|Index 1|Index A|Index 2|Index 2 A|Index 3 Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha)|Land area requirement based on P (ha) Baseline|226|452|283|565|377 Mono-AD|226|452|283|565|377 Co-AD|248|497|310|621|414 Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical N requirement (Tonne/yr) Baseline|32|82|44|108|65 Mono-AD|33|83|45|109|66 Co-AD|34|90|48|118|71 Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr)|Chemical K requirement (Tonne/yr) Baseline|19|46|21|50|24 Mono-AD|19|46|21|50|24 Co-AD|16|47|19|51|22

26%-38% at index 3 than index 1. The Baseline and Mono-AD scenarios have similar land and chemical fertilizer requirements in all cases because of the similar nutrient contents in raw PM and mono-digestate. While in Co-AD scenario, the land requirement increases by about 10% due to the additional nutrients introduced by GS, in which 42.8 ha is required to supply GS for AD. If 100% of the required P can be supplied by PM or digestate, the chemical N fertilizer requirement in Co-AD scenario increases by 6%-9% correspondingly than the Baseline, but the required chemical K decreases by 8%-16% because of the supple- ment of relatively high K content in GS. When assuming only 50% of the plant required P and 75% of required K can be supplied from PM or digestate for indexes 1 and 2 soils, the application land areas in indexes 1 A and 2 A double due to the P limit. 46: <header>4.1. Requirements of land area and chemical fertilizer </header>

Accurate land application of PM and digestate is essential, otherwise the surplus nutrients in the soil may leach to ground and surface water and cause pollution. To follow good management practice of PM and digestate, the following key factors must be considered: (1) the nutrient status of the organic fertilizer; (2) the nutrient status of the receiving soil; (3) nutrient requirements of the plant growing on the soil; and (4) the local environmental regulations for organic fertilizer application. The nutrient status of PM and digestate presented as available fertilizers are shown in Fig. 2. In Ireland, soils are classified into 4 indexes based on nutrient levels, with index 1 and 4 indicating the most barren and fertile soils, respectively. The P and K ranges under different soil indexes are shown in Table 1 [23]. As mentioned above, the PM and digestate will be applied on grassland, and the GS will be cut twice annually. The nutri- ents required for the two cuts of GS in different soil indexes are sum- marized in Table 1 [60]. The practical spreading of PM or digestate is restricted by organic fertilizer application regulations. The N applied from livestock manure must be < 170 kg N/ha/yr (Nitrates Directive 91/676/EEC). The release rates of P and K from organic manure are determined by the P and K status in soils. In Ireland, when the P status is low at index 1-2 soils, only 50% of P in organic manure is deemed
Table 1 Soil index and fertilizer requirements for grass silage.

Soil index|Index 1|Index 2|Index 3|Index 4 Soil P range (mg/L)|0.0-3.0|3.1-5.0|5.1-8.0| Soil K range (mg/L)|0-50|51-100|101-150| Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements|Fertilizer requirements N (kg/ha)|225|225|225|225 P (kg/ha)|50|40|30|0 K (kg/ha)|245|205|160|0

available in the year of application, therefore it is recommended to supply 50% of plant required P from organic manure, with the remaining 50% from chemical fertilizer [26,27]. Similarly, 75% of plant required K is recommended to be supplied by organic manure with the other 25% from chemical fertilizer [27]. Medium to high P and K status soils (index 3-4) have much lower capacity to retain P and K, and 100% of the P and K can be applied from organic manure [26,27].

The 1-4 soil indexes are used to calculate the land area required for spreading the PM or digestate. As comparison and reference for areas with no P and K application limitations, the 100% application of P and K from PM/digestate at index 1 and 2 soils are also considered. Five cases are developed based on soil index 1-3 to present how changes in soil fertility and application rates of P and K would affect the land area and chemical fertilizer requirements. The LCA analysis on 3 PM management scenarios in the previous sections are conducted based on the soil index 1, which is considered as the default case. Soil at index 4 requires zero P and K fertilizers, which is excluded in the analysis to avoid nutrient loss to the environment.

The rising in land area results in great increase in chemical fertilizers. The chemical N and K requirements increase by 36%-41% and 11%-19% at index 2, and increase by 100%-109% and

26%-38% at index 3 than index 1. The Baseline and Mono-AD scenarios have similar land and chemical fertilizer requirements in all cases because of the similar nutrient contents in raw PM and mono-digestate. While in Co-AD scenario, the land requirement increases by about 10% due to the additional nutrients introduced by GS, in which 42.8 ha is required to supply GS for AD. If 100% of the required P can be supplied by PM or digestate, the chemical N fertilizer requirement in Co-AD scenario increases by 6%-9% correspondingly than the Baseline, but the required chemical K decreases by 8%-16% because of the supple- ment of relatively high K content in GS. When assuming only 50% of the plant required P and 75% of required K can be supplied from PM or digestate for indexes 1 and 2 soils, the application land areas in indexes 1 A and 2 A double due to the P limit. It causes much greater chemical fertilizer requirement. The chemical N and K for index 1 A increase by 152%-165% and 142%-194% than index 1, and the requirements for index 2 A increase by 142%-146% and 138%-168% than index 2.
50: The increase in land application area leads to longer transportation distance, greater chemical fertilizer requirements, and more machine operation for land spreading. All these result in higher GHG emissions and greater energy consumption. Fig. 6 illustrates the GWP and FDP for the above five cases. Obviously, the Co-AD scenario performs best in both environmental categories. If 100% of the plant required P and K can be supplied by PM/digestate, the index 1 case has the lowest GWP and FDP. It means index 1 is the most suitable soil type for PM/digestate application under this circumstance. If only 50% of grass required P and 75% of required K can be supplied from PM/digestate, in Co-AD sce- nario, the GWP and FDP in index 1 A increase by 34% and 12% than index 1, respectively, and in index 2 A increases by 52% and 19% than index 2, respectively. Under this condition, P index 3 soil becomes the optimal soil type for application.

Co-AD could be considered as a preferable treatment method for PM due to its best performance in most environmental impacts assessed. However, the digestate management is a critical issue to be concerned while assessing the economic value and environmental impacts of AD. The location of AD plant, where and how to spread the digestate can significantly change the environmental impacts. Based on the above analysis, the AD plant is suggested to be built in P index 1 or 3 area depending on different organic fertilizer application regulations. The area required for digestate land application is up to 5.8 and 9.7 times of the area for providing GS (42.8 ha). In this case, digestate management should be in a higher priority rather than GS supply while considering the AD plant location. In Ireland, about 200 commercial SOW herds are operated in 2017 [28]. If Co-AD scenario is applied, 49,600 or 82,800 ha land area is required for digestate application on P index 1 or 3 soil. Grassland dominates in Ireland with an area of 4.1 million ha, which accounts for ~92% of the total agricultural area [61]. According to the national soil analysis, P index 1, 2, 3, and 4 soils account for 21%, 33%,
Fig. 6. Influences of different soil types and nutrient application rates on global warming potential (a) and fossil fuel depletion potential (b).

25%, and 21% of the grassland in Ireland, respectively [62]. The area of P index 1 and index 3 soils are 861,903 and 1,026,075 ha from the country level, respectively, which are 17 and 12 times of the required digestate application areas.

These results can provide references to the farmers, the gas industry and the policy makers. The availability of nutrients in digestate in different soil nutrient conditions must be considered by farmers in performing the best farming practice so that they can apply the right organic fertilizer usage and chemical fertilizer usage. A proper location selection of the AD plant in consideration of the soil nutrient conditions and the nutrient availability of the digestate is very important to the gas industry to reduce the digestate management costs through optimized land application area and transportation distance. The results are also referable for policy makers in maximizing the environmental and eco- nomic benefits in energy recovery from biowastes. In the Irish case, co- digestion of PM and GS is an environmentally sustainable solution.

54: In this study, static analysis is conducted on AD systems based on average annual data. However, the characteristics of feedstocks change with time, which may affect the performance of the AD systems. For example, the dry matter digestibility of GS is 75% in May but decreases

to 60% at the end of June [30]. Besides, the degradation of organic matters takes place in storage of PM and GS. All these can cause fluc- tuation to the AD systems and a dynamic simulation should be further conducted to attain more precise results.

The economic analysis of co-digestion systems has been carried out by a few researchers [6,9]. To further assess the viability of the man- ure/GS co-digestion system, a detailed cost-benefit analysis of a grazing farming system integrating grass growth for cattle grazing, co-digestion of manure and GS, application of digestate for grass growth, should be carried out. The environmental impact assessment in this study mainly focuses on GWP and FDP, but other impact categories may also be important and need further discussion.
55: All the emission factors collected in the life cycle inventory in this study are annual average values, which can be greatly affected by various factors and subsequently affect the nutrient availability. First, the land application method is one of the most important factors to affect NH3 emission. Compared with the surface broadcast, Application of manure by the injection, incorporation and band spreading methods can reduce the NH3 emissions by 80%, 70% and 55%, respectively [22]. Second, the temperature and precipitation in different seasons also have great effects on emission factors. In autumn, 55%-75% of the total N in livestock slurry/digestate is lost in forms of NH3, N2O emissions and NO3 leaching, with a grass nitrogen use efficiency of 13%-16%; while in spring, only 20%-30% of the total N is lost, and the nitrogen use effi- ciency is much higher at 41%-54% [55]. The uncounted N may be retained in the soil in an organic form. In this case, the application rates of organic and chemical fertilizers need to be balanced to optimise organic fertilizer use efficiency. Third, the soil index is not precise enough to direct smart fertilization, and the application based on the soil nutrient status of specific farms is necessary. Therefore, precise fertil- ization should be conducted, considering the land application method, season, and specific soil nutrient status to maximize the fertilizer effi- ciency and minimize negative effects to the environment.
56: This study only assessed widely used AD and land application methods due to their easy operation and economic viability. But in some areas, land shortage, local soil conditions, and environmental regula- tions do not allow land application, SO alternative methods must be sought. New technologies for PM and digestate valorisation could be assessed in the future. For example, the liquid fraction of digestate after separation, or directly diluted digestate can be used as feedstocks for microalgae cultivation, and the cultivated biomass can be further valorised to produce livestock feed, biodiesel, protein and valuable chemicals [63,64]. The PM hydrolysate or digestate can also be used to recover nutrients (ammonium, phosphate and potassium) with newly developed technologies [65-68]; this will reduce the nutrient loading on the applied land. The solid fraction of the digestate can be pyrolyzed for biochar production using the surplus heat in the AD plant, and the product can be used as absorbent and soil conditioner [20]. The high contents of lignin, cellulose and hemi-cellulose in GS hinder its biode- gradability and lead to long retention time during AD. After thermal-chemical or thermal-physical pre-treatment, the liquid fraction of the GS could be extracted for AD to significantly reduce retention time, while the solid fraction can be used for pyrolysis biochar pro- duction [69-71]. While, most of these valorisation technologies are in early development stages and more research should be conducted for developing these technologies to practical use.
57: This study assesses the validity of PM mono-digestion and PM/GS co- digestion as alternative PM management methods to direct land appli- cation. The assessed issues include potential environmental impacts and

PM/digestate land application for nutrient recycling. Instead of using universal IPCC parameters, a detailed practical emission inventory was collected, and a more accurate and comprehensive PM management assessment system was established. Research results can confirm the hypothesis: PM and GS co-digestion can be considered as a sustainable management method, which reduces the GWP and FDP significantly due to the increased biomethane production. Environmental impacts of co- digestion of GS and PM are seriously impacted by the soil nutrient conditions, regional environmental regulations, and crop nutrient re- quirements, which determines the organic fertilizer application rate, land area, and transport distance. This research can provide advice to farmers about the usage of organic fertilizer on land (digestate and raw manure), and insights to the gas industry and policy makers to deter- mine a cost-effective and environmentally friendly AD plant location and maximize the environmental benefits. Perspectives are given for further research.
